title: The Bursting Of The Boom
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shipping-office clerks are ן¿½short,ן¿½ the manager is gruff,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They cannot make reductions,ן¿½ and ן¿½the fares are low enough.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They ship us West with cattle, and we go like cattle too;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fight like dogs three times a day for what we get to chew....

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll have the pick of empty bunks and lots of stretching room,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And go for next to nothing at the Bursting of the Boom.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So wait till the Boom bursts!, weן¿½ll all get a show:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then when the Boom bursts is our time to go.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll meet ן¿½em coming back in shoals, with looks of deepest gloom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But weן¿½re the sort that battle through at the Bursting of the Boom.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The captainן¿½s easy-going when Fremantle comes in sight;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He canן¿½t say when youן¿½ll get ashore, perhaps tomorrow night;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your coins are few, the charges high; you must not linger here,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½ll get your boxes from the hold when sheן¿½s ן¿½longside the pier.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The launch will foul the gangway, and the trembling bulwarks loom
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Above a fleet of harbour craft, at the Bursting of the Boom.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So wait till the Boom bursts!, weן¿½ll all get a show;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½ll ן¿½take you for a bob, sir,ן¿½ and where you want to go.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½ll ן¿½take the big portmanteau, sir, if he might so presumeן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You neednן¿½t hump your luggage at the Bursting of the Boom.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s loafers, Customs-loafers, and you pay and pay again;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They hinder you and cheat you from the gangway to the train;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The pubs and restaurants are full, they havenן¿½t room for more;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They charge us each three shillings for a shakedown on the floor;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, ן¿½Show this gentleman upstairs, the first front parlour room.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Weן¿½ll see about your luggage, sirן¿½, at the Bursting of the Boom.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So wait till the Boom bursts!, weן¿½ll all get a show;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wait till the Boom bursts, and swear mighty low.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We mostly charge a pound a week. How do you like the room?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½Show this gentleman the bathן¿½, at the Bursting of the Boom.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I go down to the timber-yard (I cannot face the rent)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To get some strips of oregon to frame my hessian tent;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To buy some scraps of lumber for a table or a shelf:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The boss comes up and says I might just look round for myself ;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The foreman grunts and turns away as silent as the tomb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The boss himself will wait on me at the Bursting of the Boom.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So wait till the Boom bursts!, weן¿½ll all get a load.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You had better take those scraps, sir, theyן¿½re only in the road.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now, where the hellן¿½s the carter?ן¿½ youן¿½ll hear the foreman fume;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, ן¿½Take that timber round at once!ן¿½ at the Bursting of the Boom.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each one-a-penny grocer, in his box of board and tin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will think it condescending to consent to take you in;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And not content with twice as much as what is just and right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They charge and cheat you doubly, for the Boom is at its height.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½sן¿½Take it now or leave it now;ן¿½ ן¿½your money or your room;ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But ן¿½Whoן¿½s attending Mr. Brown?ן¿½ at the Bursting of the Boom.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So wait till the Boom bursts!, and take what you can get,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thereן¿½s not the slightest hurry, and your bill ainן¿½t ready yet.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½ll call and get your orders until the crack oן¿½ doom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And send them round directly, at the Bursting of the Boom.


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No Country and no Brotherhood, such things are dead and cold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A camp from all the lands or none, all mad for love of gold ;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where Tן¿½othersider number one makes slave of number two,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the vilest women of the world the vilest ways pursue;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And men go out and slave and bake and die in agony
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In western hells that God forgot, where never man should be.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I feel a prophet in my heart that speaks the one word ן¿½Doom!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And aye youן¿½ll hear the Devil laugh at the Bursting of the Boom.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
